retrieved_docs.title: Short-Term Memory Impairment -- Pathophysiology -- The Amnestic Syndrome
retrieved_docs.content: The amnestic syndrome is an impairment in the ability to form new memories. Regardless of its etiology, the memories of recent events are particularly vulnerable, whereas long-term memory is more resilient and protected from damage. This concept is not new; towards the end of the 19th  century, the French psychologist Theodule-Armand Ribot (1839-1916) demonstrated that amnesia affects memories in reverse order of their development. [37] This idea underscores the importance of distinguishing between anterograde and retrograde amnesia. The former concerns the new memories, whereas the latter is well-established by neural networks. This distinction explains why some conditions involving  drugs, such as benzodiazepines, can lead to a deficit, especially within short-term memory. In turn, amnestic disorders primarily affect anterograde memories. A recent study on short-term memory binding has identified amnestic mild cognitive impairment as the most typical preclinical stage of Alzheimer disease. Neuropsychological measures are relatively sensitive and specific diagnostic tools for amnestic mild cognitive impairment, although they do not replace the utility of biomarkers. [38]

retrieved_docs.title: Short-Term Memory Impairment -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Among other causes of memory impairment, a common adverse effect of electroconvulsive therapy is short-term memory alteration during treatment. Another notable concern is medication-induced memory loss. The list of drugs implicated includes benzodiazepines, antiepileptic drugs, opioids, and tricyclic antidepressants. [25] [26] [27] Limited evidence, primarily from observational data and case reports, is available regarding statin use and memory loss. Most of these drugs, such as benzodiazepines, impair memory processing and, in turn, can present an obstacle to the consolidation of information. [28] [29]

retrieved_docs.title: Short-Term Memory Impairment -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Other conditions that impair memory tasks include alcohol and drug abuse, such as marijuana; heavy cigarette smoking; sleep deprivation; severe stress; and vitamin B12 deficiency. Prolonged alcohol intake can lead to Korsakoff syndrome, which is a complex amnestic disorder with neuropsychological sequelae caused by vitamin B1 (thiamine) deficiency. In addition to alcohol, other causes can lead to vitamin B1 deficiency (non-alcoholic Korsakoff syndrome) with related memory disorders, including dietary deficiencies, prolonged vomiting, and eating disorders. Korsakoff-like amnestic syndromes have also been observed after brain lesions involving the anteromedian thalamus and hippocampus. [23]

retrieved_docs.title: Short-Term Memory Impairment -- Review Questions
retrieved_docs.content: Access free multiple choice questions on this topic. Click here for a simplified version. Comment on this article.

retrieved_docs.title: Aducanumab -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Disease onset is insidious, causing a gradual decline of behavioral and cognitive abilities that manifest as an initial short-term memory impairment accompanied eventually by impaired language, attention, comprehension, and executive functioning. [1] Current symptomatic management of Alzheimer disease entails treatment with cholinesterase inhibitors and N -methyl-D-aspartate (NMDA) antagonists, as there is no cure or curative treatment to date. [1] [6] The presence of 1 or 2 APOE ε4 alleles increases the risk for AD development.

retrieved_docs.title: Short-Term Memory Impairment -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Short-term memory impairment involves forgetting information to which the patient has been recently exposed. An individual with signs of losing short-term memory asks the same questions repeatedly, forgets immediate location, forgets recent events, or forgets something they just saw or read. The loss of immediate memory is also termed fixation amnesia. The clinical features of short-term memory impairment are variable and depend on the underlying cause of the memory alteration. Within short-term memory, different memory domains, such as verbal or visuospatial components, and in different degrees, can be altered. In most cases, memory alterations are blurred and last as long as the pathology that caused them or resolved over weeks or months, for example, postoperative cognitive dysfunction.

retrieved_docs.title: Short-Term Memory Impairment -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: A recent study has outlined screening procedures for early Alzheimer disease to identify patients who fulfill clinical and biomarker criteria efficiently. These procedures include performance on the Free and Cued Selective Reminding Test (FCSRT) and Repeatable Battery for the Assessment of Neuropsychological Status (RBANS). [53] Further diagnostic examinations must focus on the cause of memory decline. Instrumental investigations, such as magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) or computed tomography (CT) scan, or laboratory examinations, such as vitamin B12, may be necessary. Previous radiologic studies have described the role of MRI in ruling out underlying causes of cognitive deterioration and revealing patterns of atrophy with a predictive value for certain types of dementias. Although these patterns are not specific or unique to each disease, they can help confirm diagnostic suspicion or identify certain processes. [54]

retrieved_docs.title: Short-Term Memory Impairment -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: Depending on its severity, short-term memory loss can lead to complications, significantly impacting the patient's social interactions. These complications may manifest as missing appointments, forgetting conversations, and a decline in communication with friends and family, resulting in withdrawal from the community without active support. In the latter stages, patients may struggle to complete complex tasks of daily living, such as grocery shopping and bill payments, requiring additional assistance. In severe cases of memory loss, patients can be a danger to themselves, such as forgetting to turn off the stove, getting lost in familiar places, and being unable to find their way home. When coupled with comorbid medical conditions, patients may forget to take medications or forget that they have already taken medications; hence, they may take multiple doses of their medications, leading to further complications. Activities of daily living are also compromised as the patient may forget to eat, bathe, or have difficulty dressing. Eventually, patients may become entirely dependent on others and may require relocation to a nursing home for adequate care.

retrieved_docs.title: Short-Term Memory Impairment -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Several strategies have been proposed to enhance memory tasks, including behavioral and non-behavioral approaches. Behavioral approaches, or cognitive training, are mostly focused on the maintenance rehearsal, which facilitates memory processing through repetitive stimuli, and the elaborative rehearsal, which entails associating new information with stored knowledge and analyzing it. Several proposed behavioral strategies include the combination of Tai Chi movements and breathing. However, further research and controlled studies are required to confirm their real benefit. Concerning pharmacological approaches, although commonly prescribed, drugs approved to manage memory symptoms of Alzheimer disease, such as cholinesterase inhibitors, have not demonstrated significant benefits in slowing down or preventing the progression of mild cognitive impairment to Alzheimer disease. [55] [56] [57]

retrieved_docs.title: Short-Term Memory Impairment -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Dementia with Lewy bodies is a neurodegenerative disease characterized by cognitive, behavioral, and motor symptoms. However, it presents a more challenging clinical management and a poorer prognosis compared to other forms of dementia. Frontotemporal dementia is the second most common presenile dementia, manifested as prominent behavioral, language, and cognitive impairment. This type of dementia has a vital genetic component contributing to the pathogenesis. Progressive supranuclear palsy is a progressive neurodegenerative tauopathy that presents with motor, behavioral, and cognitive dysfunction. This disease is atypical parkinsonism with prominent 4R-tau neuropathology, and the typical clinical manifestations include early postural instability or akinetic-rigid syndrome, unprovoked falls, cognitive decline, and prominent eye movement abnormalities that consist of saccadic slowing, followed by gaze limitation or vertical supranuclear gaze palsy.

retrieved_docs.title: Short-Term Memory Impairment -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Apart from cognitive training, non-behavioral strategies concern transcranial direct current stimulation in short-term memory impairment due to primary progressive aphasia. In contrast, current research is being conducted on the effects of nutraceuticals, such as thymoquinone or green tea, against Alzheimer disease–related short-term memory decline and tryptophan-tyrosine–related peptides, such as lactolin, to mitigate stress-induced memory alterations. [64] [65] [66]  Although several studies have shown that Ginkgo biloba can improve the risk of short-term memory impairment, the scientific evidence is limited. [67]

retrieved_docs.title: Short-Term Memory Impairment -- Introduction -- The Role of the Hippocampal Network
retrieved_docs.content: Hippocampal neurogenesis regulates the maintenance of long-term potentiation. [10] However, the hippocampal network, including the parahippocampal gyrus, hippocampus, and neocortical areas, is not the storage site for memories but plays a crucial role in forming new memories and their subsequent reactivation. The hippocampus appears to have a limited capacity but acquires information quickly and automatically. Over time, the initially available information is permanent in other brain structures, particularly the cortex), independently from the activity of the hippocampus itself. The crucial mechanism of this transfer involves the reactivation (replay) of neural activity configurations.

retrieved_docs.title: Mild Cognitive Impairment -- Histopathology -- Synaptic Degeneration in the Hippocampus
retrieved_docs.content: The hippocampus shows reduced synaptic plasticity in MCI. [20] The number of synapses is reduced in MCI when compared to controls with NCI, and people with early AD have a significantly lower number of synapses when compared with MCI and NCI groups. The number of synapses also correlated with MMSE and delayed recall scores. [27]

retrieved_docs.title: Short-Term Memory Impairment -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: According to a recent study, around 5% to 15% of individuals with mild cognitive impairment progress to dementia annually. The prevalence of dementia in Blacks is almost twice compared to that of Whites. [32] [33] In China, the population older than 60 is expected to increase approximately from 12% in 2010 to 33% of the overall population by 2050. Therefore, the increase in the aging population is projected to result in an Alzheimer disease prevalence of 27.7 million people in China by 2050. [34] Parkinson disease affects approximately 1% of the population older than 60, whereas the incidence of Huntington disease is 0.38 per 1,00,000 per year. [35] [36] For non-neurodegenerative conditions, estimating epidemiological data is challenging as memory decline is not always in the clinical picture.

retrieved_docs.title: Charles Bonnet Syndrome -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Charles Bonnet syndrome (CBS) is a phenomenon characterized by complex visual hallucinations in visually challenged patients but otherwise psychologically normal. The phenomenon was first described in 1760 by Swiss philosopher Charles Bonnet who observed this phenomenon in his grandfather, who was visually impaired due to cataracts. In this phenomenon, the hallucinations have been described as formed, vivid, of realistic objects or people, and they tend to recur. In CBS, the patient is aware that the image is not real and may see these images as disturbing. However, hallucinations can also fit in the visual phenomenon and may be indiscernible from real objects. Hallucinations are described as the perception of unrealistic images, sounds, or smells in the absence of any external stimulus. Hallucinations in older people are usually markers of underlying neurological disorders like Alzheimer disease, parkinsonism, delirium, sensory deprivation, insomnia, or psychosis. The other type of hallucination is release hallucination which usually originates from the external world but is experienced when there is an inadequate stimulus to the sensory system. The hallucinations in CBS are visual and complex but are not disturbing. CBS can also be a marker of dementia in elderly patients. CBS can manifest when there is a lesion at any level of the visual system, and typically patients manifest with a loss of central visual acuity. CBS is also associated with ARMD with a secondary decline in visual acuity. This activity deals with the etiology, epidemiology, and pathophysiology of CBS and highlights the role of the interprofessional team in the investigation and treatment of CBS.

retrieved_docs.title: Bacopa monnieri -- Indications -- Animal Studies
retrieved_docs.content: In a study on male albino mice, Bacopa monniera extract was administered along with phenytoin in the second week of a 2-week trial. There was a significant reversal of phenytoin-induced cognitive impairment with improved acquisition and retention of memory and no effect on the anticonvulsant activity. [16] Some animal studies suggest a benefit from Bacopa monnieri for Alzheimer disease, [3] [17] epilepsy, [18] [19] Parkinson disease, [20] [21] and cerebral ischemia/infarct. [22] [23] [24] It has been shown to cure gastric ulcers in animals and have anti- Helicobacter pylori activity in human colonic tissue. [25] [26]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 2 -- Community 1063
retrieved_docs.content: Feigenbaum Bergeron Richardson syndrome is characterized by impaired interpretation, which manifests as dementia, senile changes, acute confusional states, and a practical social communication disorder (اضطراب التواصل الاجتماعي (العملي)). This impairment of interpretive function is also linked to chronic kidney failure (قصور كلوي مزمن). In addition, glomerulonephritis (التهاب كبيبات الكلى) is clinically associated with chronic kidney failure, underscoring a connection between renal pathology and the cognitive and communicative deficits observed in this syndrome.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 3 -- Community 1751
retrieved_docs.content: Feigenbaum Bergeron Richardson syndrome is linked to an impaired ability to interpret information, as reflected by the concept of “interpretation_of.” This impairment is manifested in several clinical contexts: it is associated with dementia, senile type with an acute confused state, it is interpreted as اضطراب التواصل الاجتماعي (العملي) (social communication disorder, practical), and it also relates to قصور كلوي مزمن (chronic kidney failure). The syndrome’s effect on interpretation therefore connects neurocognitive decline, acute confusion, functional communication deficits, and renal insufficiency, underscoring the importance of integrated evaluation and management of these interrelated conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2473
retrieved_docs.content: Feigenbaum Bergeron Richardson syndrome is associated with a marked impairment in interpretation functions. This impaired interpretation of information contributes to cognitive disturbances that can present as dementia, senile decline, and acute confused states (verwardheidstoestand). In addition, the syndrome’s effect on interpretive abilities extends to the social domain, manifesting as a practical social communication disorder (اضطراب التواصل الاجتماعي (العملي)). Recognizing that Feigenbaum Bergeron Richardson syndrome leads to impaired interpretation of both cognitive and social cues highlights the need for comprehensive assessment of memory, orientation, and communicative competence in patients with this condition.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 3 -- Community 2574
retrieved_docs.content: Emotional blunting (تبلد المشاعر) is identified as a clinical manifestation of specific brain atrophy (ضمور المخ المحدد). This relationship indicates that patients who exhibit localized cerebral atrophy may present with reduced emotional responsiveness, and recognizing emotional blunting as a sign of such atrophy can aid clinicians in diagnosing underlying neurological conditions where affective changes are a prominent feature.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 2 -- Community 1005
retrieved_docs.content: Encephalitis is a condition that occurs in the brain (mozek). The brain serves as the anatomical site for several pathological findings, including metabolic brain disease (اعتلال دماغي أيضي) and brain compression (انْضِغاطُ الدِّماغ). It is also the location where cerebral edema (أوديما دماغية), general brain disease (اعتلال دماغي), and toxic brain disease (اعتلال دماغي سمي) can develop. Functionally, the brain is the locus of the cognitive process of thinking (myšlení) and is linked to the emotional experience of guilt (الشعور بالذنب). Clinically, the brain is the direct target for electroconvulsive therapy (العلاج بالتخليج الكهربي). This integrated view underscores the brain’s central role in disease manifestation, neurocognitive functions, emotional processing, and therapeutic interventions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 2 -- Community 1079
retrieved_docs.content: Encephalitis is described as being located in the brain (mozek). The brain itself serves as the anatomical site for several pathological findings, including metabolic brain disorder (اعتلال دماغي أيضي) and brain compression (انْضِغاطُ الدِّماغ). In addition, the brain is identified as the location of toxic brain disorder (اعتلال دماغي سمي). Beyond structural pathology, the brain is also linked to the psychological process of feeling guilt (الشعور بالذنب), indicating its role in emotional and cognitive functions. Therapeutically, the brain is the direct procedural site for electroconvulsive therapy (العلاج بالتخليج الكهربي), a treatment that targets neural activity. Together, these relationships highlight the brain’s central involvement in infectious, metabolic, compressive, toxic, and emotional conditions, as well as its importance as a target for specific neuropsychiatric interventions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 3 -- Community 2397
retrieved_docs.content: The brain (mozek) is the anatomical location for a range of pathological findings and a key physiological process. Encephalitis is located in the brain. The brain serves as the finding site for a brain cyst (كيسة دماغية), a metabolic brain disorder (اعتلال دماغي أيضي), brain compression (انْضِغاطُ الدِّماغ), and a toxic brain disorder (اعتلال دماغي سمي). In addition, the brain is the site of the biological process of thinking (myšlení). This underscores the brain’s central role in both disease manifestation and cognitive function, highlighting its importance in medical evaluation and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2225
retrieved_docs.content: Encephalitis is a condition that localizes to the brain (mozek). The brain serves as the finding site for several neurological disorders, including metabolic brain disease (اعتلال دماغي أيضي), brain compression (انْضِغاطُ الدِّماغ), and toxic brain disease (اعتلال دماغي سمي). It is also the anatomical location where the cognitive process of thinking (myšlení) occurs and is linked to the emotional experience of guilt (الشعور بالذنب). Moreover, the brain is the direct target for therapeutic interventions such as electroconvulsive therapy (العلاج بالتخليج الكهربي).

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 2 -- Community 868
retrieved_docs.content: Encephalitis, an inflammation of the brain, is situated in the organ referred to as “mozek” (the brain). The brain serves as the finding site for several neurological conditions, including اعتلال دماغي أيضي (a metabolic brain disorder), انْضِغاطُ الدِّماغ (brain compression), and اعتلال دماغي سمي (a toxic brain disorder). In addition to being the anatomical substrate for disease, mozek is the location where the biological process of myšlení (thinking) takes place, and it is also linked to the emotional experience of الشعور بالذنب (feeling guilt). Recognizing that encephalitis and these various disorders, cognitive functions, and emotions all converge on the brain underscores its central role in both pathology and normal mental processes, which is essential for comprehensive patient assessment and care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 3 -- Community 1368
retrieved_docs.content: Anti‑dementia drugs are classified as a subclass of psychoanaleptics, meaning they belong to the broader group of central‑nervous‑system stimulants that enhance mental activity. This classification reflects the pharmacologic action of anti‑dementia agents, which aim to improve cognition and counteract the decline associated with dementia by stimulating neural pathways, a property shared with other psychoanaleptic medications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2628
retrieved_docs.content: Feigenbaum Bergeron Richardson syndrome is linked to an impairment in interpretation, a deficit that is expressed as اضطراب التواصل الاجتماعي (العملي) (practical social‑communication disorder) and also interferes with the interpretation of قصور كلوي مزمن (chronic kidney failure). Chronic kidney failure itself is clinically associated with التهاب كبيبات الكلى (glomerulonephritis). Consequently, the syndrome’s impact on interpretive abilities may affect both social communication and the clinical understanding of renal pathology, highlighting the importance of addressing interpretive deficits in patients who present with these overlapping neurological and nephrological conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 4 -- Community 2790
retrieved_docs.content: Feigenbaum Bergeron Richardson syndrome is characterized by an impairment in interpretation, a deficit that extends to both neurobehavioral and systemic domains. This impaired interpretive ability is specifically linked to اضطراب التواصل الاجتماعي (العملي), a practical form of social communication disorder, indicating that individuals with the syndrome may experience difficulties in processing and responding to social cues in everyday interactions. In addition, the same interpretive impairment affects the understanding of قصور كلوي مزمن (chronic kidney failure), suggesting that the syndrome can also hinder accurate perception and management of renal disease-related information. Together, these relationships highlight how Feigenbaum Bergeron Richardson syndrome disrupts cognitive interpretation, leading to challenges in social communication and the comprehension of chronic kidney pathology, which are important considerations for comprehensive patient assessment and multidisciplinary care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 0 -- Community 2
retrieved_docs.content: “non‑infectious encephalitis” SMQ, connecting sleep‑wake dysregulation to inflammatory brain processes. Apathy belongs to the “non‑infectious delirium/brain disease” SMQ, which additionally includes attention‑deficit, concentration weakness and early daytime sleepiness, linking cognitive‑motor deficits with delirium‑type brain disease. Gender identity is associated with stereotyped repetitive movements; together with emotional blunting, these signs are interpreted as clinical expressions of localized brain atrophy. Miscellaneous mental‑health disorders are broadly related to an unspecified transient organic mental disorder and are primarily mapped from organic mood disorder, indicating an organic substrate for diverse psychiatric presentations. A confusional state without acute features and an organic hallucination syndrome are members of the “anticholinergic syndrome” SMQ, reflecting anticholinergic toxicity as a cause of acute psychotic‑like states. Schizophrenic mania (“hous

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 3 -- Community 1519
retrieved_docs.content: Chronic inflammatory small bowel disease is characterized by a chronic clinical course, which can progress to secondary diabetes mellitus accompanied by diabetic chronic kidney disease and may also culminate in chronic kidney failure (قصور كلوي مزمن). Feigenbaum Bergeron Richardson syndrome impairs the ability to interpret information; this impairment is associated with a practical social communication disorder (اضطراب التواصل الاجتماعي (العملي)) and extends to a distorted interpretation of chronic kidney failure. Together, these relationships highlight how a persistent inflammatory bowel condition can predispose patients to metabolic and renal complications, while Feigenbaum Bergeron Richardson syndrome contributes to cognitive‑communication deficits that may further affect the perception and management of renal disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 1 -- Community 59
retrieved_docs.content: be employed to treat the seizure condition described in Arabic as “نوبة بداية عدم الحركة المعممة,” which falls under the broader categories of epilepsy and convulsions. Characteristic findings for epilepsy‑related convulsions are typically localized to the velký mozek (the cerebrum). Collectively, this class provides multiple mechanism‑specific options for individualized management of diverse epilepsy forms while emphasizing the importance of awareness of adverse effects, accurate data representation, and the neuroanatomical context of convulsive events.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 1 -- Community 157
retrieved_docs.content: impairment (خلل في الانتباه), memory impairment, psychological distress, reduced concentration (ضعف التركيز), blunted affect (عاطفة متقلصة), hyperkinetic activity (فرط النشاط الحركي), muscle hyperactivity (فرط النشاط العضلي), primary excessive daytime sleepiness (فرط النوم في النهار الأولي), anhedonia, and loss of ambition, all of which require comprehensive assessment for impact on daily functioning. Alcohol‑related disorders are clinically associated with non‑infectious delirium/brain disorder (الهذيان/الاعتلال الدماغي غير المعدي (SMQ)) and may be treated with prazepam. Hypokalemia syndrome is linked to chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (مرض الانسداد الرئوي المزمن). Clorazepate (كلورازيبات) is noted as an adjunctive agent to alleviate withdrawal symptoms and improve psychiatric stability. The classification “diseases and injuries” includes malaise and fatigue, mapped to feeling of weakness (الشعور بالضعف), fatigue (النوام), and broader neurogenic weakness (الضعف العصبي).
